message.content: Adversaries can steal application access tokens to gain unauthorized access to remote systems and resources, often leading to privilege escalation and further compromise. These tokens, commonly used in cloud, container-based applications, and SaaS, allow adversaries to perform actions with the permissions of the compromised accounts. OAuth is a frequently exploited framework for issuing these tokens. In Kubernetes environments, attackers can steal service account tokens from compromised containers to access Kubernetes API commands. Token theft can occur through social engineering, such as spearphishing, where users are tricked into granting access to malicious applications. Adversaries may also steal refresh tokens to maintain long-term access. Notable groups like APT28 have used fake applications to steal OAuth tokens from services like Gmail and Yahoo. Mitigation strategies include implementing strong authentication mechanisms, monitoring for unusual token usage, and educating users about phishing risks. Regularly rotating tokens and limiting their lifespan can also reduce the impact of token theft.
